petitioner_background: The petitioner is a Korean opera singer (soprano) seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the INA. She earned a Bachelor of Music from Seoul National University (1999), a Master of Music from the College-Conservatory of Music at University of Cincinnati (2001), and an Artist Diploma in Performance from the Julliard School (2003). The petition was filed on February 21, 2007. She has performed in various operas, recitals, and concerts in Europe, Asia, and the United States, and participated in several young artist programs and masterclasses.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) Thierry Mermod Prize (2,500 Swiss Francs) from Verbier Festival & Academy 2004, received as a participant in the Voice Masterclass from July 16 - August 1, 2004; (2) 'First in Her Class' and 'Musical Artist of the Year Award for the Year 1998' from Seoul National University; (3) 'Musical Artist of the Year Award for the Year 1998' and 'Valedictorian Scholarship Award to the Julliard School of Music for the Year 2002' from the Artist Scholarship Foundation for the Republic of Korea; (4) Certificate as '1st Runner Up' in the Westminster Choir College of Rider University Graduate Voice Competition; (5) October 16, 2002 letter from Julliard indicating her name was submitted as a nominee for the sixth annual Alice Tully Vocal Arts Debut Recital; (6) Certificate of Award from Korean International Music Foundation (KIMF) 'Music Competition 2005' placing first in the Voice Division at Zipper Concert Hall in Los Angeles; (7) Unsigned certificate from Les Azuriales Opera Festival for Mark and Sarah Holford Award for Best Performance of the Evening at International Summer Academy of Nice Concert of Soloists 2004; (8) Claim of first prize at Biot competition (June 2004) mentioned in Nice-Matin article, with brochure for 21st Festival of Music Hours of Biot submitted on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not meet this criterion. For the Thierry Mermod Prize, there was no indication she faced competition from throughout her field rather than being limited to participating masterclass students in her approximate age group at early career stages, and it did not constitute a major internationally recognized award. The Seoul National University and Korean scholarship awards lacked certified English translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3); the SNU award reflected institutional recognition from her alma mater, and the scholarships represented financial assistance for studies rather than recognition for excellence in the field. University study is training for future employment, not a field of endeavor, and student competition honors do not distinguish her from others in the field. The Westminster 1st Runner Up recognition lacked evidence from organizers that it was a prize or award, and the competition was limited to graduate students. The Alice Tully nomination only reflected her being nominated, not winning, and was institutional recognition from her music school. The KIMF award lacked evidence of national or international recognition and there was no evidence it was open to experienced professionals rather than limited to young artists of Korean ethnicity at early careers. The Mark and Sarah Holford Award was for best student performer in an educational program with no evidence of national/international recognition and no indication of competition from throughout her field. For the Biot competition prize, there was no primary evidence from the organizer, the brochure lacked certified translation, petitioner's name didn't appear in the brochure, and there was no evidence of significance/magnitude. The AAO cited Matter of Price noting even major league athletes don't automatically meet the extraordinary ability standard, and success in student/age-restricted competitions does not qualify. No evidence established the awards had significant national or international recognition beyond the events' contexts or that competitions were open to well-established vocalists of all ages.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) Several playbills for various concerts and productions; (2) August 11, 2004 article in Nice-Matin titled 'The Summer Academy ends in song' with one sentence mentioning petitioner and a photo with five other individuals; (3) May 11, 2001 article in the Opera Review section of the Cincinnati Post titled 'CCM's magical Rusalka prevails in real-life drama' with six sentences discussing her performance; (4) May 13, 2001 article in Opera Review section of Cincinnati Enquirer titled 'CCM triumphs in first performance of Rusalka' with two sentences discussing her performance; (5) Article titled 'Rising star in music' in Los Angeles edition of Korea Daily; (6) Two articles in New York edition of Korea Daily in October 2002, one titled 'Big success in young artists concert'; (7) On appeal, information from CareerBuilder about Cincinnati Enquirer readership (Sunday 808,500; weekday 562,100) and New York Times article about JoonAng Ilbo having 2 million circulation.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not meet this criterion. Playbills do not meet the requirements of title, date, and author. The Nice-Matin article was about the International Summer Academy and upcoming student concert rather than the petitioner, and there was no evidence the local newspaper qualified as major media. The Cincinnati Post article focused primarily on the Rusalka production rather than the petitioner, and without circulation statistics, the now-defunct local newspaper did not qualify as major media. The Cincinnati Enquirer article was not about the petitioner (only two sentences) and lacked evidence of distribution relative to other U.S. newspapers to qualify as 'major' media. The Korea Daily articles lacked certified full English translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), did not identify authors or publication dates for one article, and even if JoonAng Ilbo has 2 million circulation in Korea, the record did not establish that the Los Angeles and New York editions were distributed beyond the Korean-speaking segment of those two cities. Acclaim cannot come from publications in a language the vast majority of the country's population cannot comprehend.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted numerous letters of recommendation praising her talent as a vocalist, including letters from: individuals at the Metropolitan Opera House describing her as 'a highly talented young artist of great potential' with 'beautiful, warm soprano voice'; accompanist from International Vocal Arts Institute in Italy; faculty at Julliard School describing her as having 'a beautiful lyric soprano voice of unusual timber and range'; faculty from Seoul National University; and a letter discussing her participation in a Resident Artist Program partnership with Metropolitan Opera Guild and Mannes, where she was one of several sopranos chosen and her contribution was 'significant in the field of education.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not meet this criterion. Talent in one's field is not necessarily indicative of artistic contributions of major significance. The letters discussed the petitioner's progress, growth, and future potential rather than addressing how her past musical achievements qualified as contributions of major significance. A petitioner cannot file based on expectation of future eligibility, citing Matter of Katigbak. The letters did not specify exactly what her original contributions in opera have been or how they were of major significance. The phrase 'major significance' must not be superfluous. The record did not indicate the extent of petitioner's influence on other vocalists nationally or internationally, nor show the field changed as a result of her work. Letters from experts solicited in support of a petition are of less weight than preexisting, independent evidence of original contributions, citing Matter of Caron International. USCIS may evaluate the content of such letters as to whether they support eligibility. Without extensive documentation showing her work has been unusually influential or highly acclaimed, she could not meet the criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted evidence of her performances in a university setting and in conjunction with various workshops such as the Verbier Festival & Academy and the Les Azuriales International Summer Academy.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO withdrew the director's finding that the petitioner met this criterion. There was no evidence the performances were consistent with sustained national or international acclaim at the very top of her field. More fundamentally, the plain language of this regulatory criterion indicates it is intended for visual artists (such as sculptors and painters) rather than for vocalists. In the performing arts, acclaim is generally not established by the mere act of appearing in public, but by attracting a substantial audience. The regulations establish separate criteria for those whose work is in the performing arts. The petitioner's stage performances are more relevant to the 'commercial successes in the performing arts' criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(x).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) Letter from Susie Ibarra stating petitioner performed in a lead role, Noe, in the premiere of music and libretto of Shangri-la at Passage Theatre in New Jersey and South Oxford Theatre in Brooklyn; (2) Evidence of performances in a university setting and in conjunction with various international workshops; (3) January 25, 2007 letter from counsel stating petitioner performed leading roles in numerous national and international artistic performances, operas, and recital appearances; (4) In response to RFE, July 9, 2008 letter from Pre-College Division of Julliard School stating petitioner was hired as a faculty member to teach voice to non-majors, with One-Year Contract Addendum dated August 23, 2007; (5) On appeal, second letter from Julliard and Westminster Choir College regarding her role as voice teacher for pre-college students; (6) Letter from International Summer Academy of Nice stating from 2004 to 2008 petitioner was employed as assistant to a renowned singing professor teaching vocal technique to students from 50 countries.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not meet this criterion. Even if an opera production equates to an organization/establishment (which the AAO did not concede), there was no evidence Shangri-la or the venues had distinguished reputations. Performances, opera productions, recital appearances, and concerts do not constitute 'organizations or establishments.' There was no evidence her productions had distinguished national reputations or evidence differentiating her role from other performers — no evidence her name frequently received top billing or that productions' popularity increased when she performed. The Julliard faculty appointment post-dated the petition filing date, and a petitioner must establish eligibility at time of filing per Matter of Katigbak; the AAO would not consider her role as Julliard faculty. Regarding the International Summer Academy of Nice, there was no evidence her role as 'assistant' in a summer music workshop was leading or critical to the Academy as a whole, and aside from self-serving promotional material and a local newspaper article, there was no evidence the Academy had a distinguished reputation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner indicated on Form I-140 that her wages per week are $4,615 +/- and annual income is $240,000 +/-. A bank statement was submitted showing petitioner's father has two Certificates of Deposit worth a total of $177,432.43. A contract addendum was submitted showing petitioner was to be paid at the rate of $52 per hour for teaching at Julliard.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concurred with the director's findings that petitioner did not meet this criterion. The record did not include evidence (such as payroll records, Form W-2, or income tax returns) showing actual earnings for any specific period. Going on record without supporting documentary evidence is insufficient, citing Matter of Soffici. The bank CDs belonged to her father with no evidence she earned the money or how long it took. The One-Year Contract Addendum for Julliard dated August 23, 2007 post-dated the petition filing and could not be considered per Matter of Katigbak. The plain language of the criterion requires evidence of high salary 'in relation to others in the field' — petitioner offered no basis for comparison showing her compensation was significantly high compared to others in her field. Without a proper basis for comparison and objective evidence of actual earnings during a sustained period predating the petition, the petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted evidence indicating she participated in various opera productions, recitals, concerts, and workshop performances.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not meet this criterion. The criterion requires evidence of commercial successes in the form of 'sales' or 'receipts.' Simply submitting evidence of participation in various productions cannot meet the plain language of the criterion. The record did not include documented sales or receipts showing commercial successes consistent with sustained national or international acclaim. There was no evidence her performances consistently drew record crowds, were regular sell-out performances, or resulted in greater audiences than similar performances without her. No evidence showed her musical recordings generated substantial national or international sales.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO concurred with the director's finding that the petitioner failed to demonstrate receipt of a major, internationally recognized award, and failed to meet at least three of the regulatory criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). The conclusion reached by considering the evidence for each criterion separately was consistent with review of the evidence in the aggregate — even in the aggregate, the evidence did not distinguish the petitioner as one of the small percentage at the very top of the field. While the petitioner earned respect and admiration and possesses great talent as a vocalist, she had not amassed a record of accomplishment placing her among that small percentage at the very top of her field, and had not sustained national or international acclaim at this stage of her musical career. The evidence was not persuasive that her achievements set her significantly above almost all others in her field at a national or international level.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Commr. 1994)
Matter of Racine, 1995 WL 153319 (N.D. Ill. Feb. 16, 1995)
Grimson v. INS, No. 93 C 3354 (N.D. Ill. September 9, 1993)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45 (Regl. Commr. 1971)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791 (Commr. 1988)
Janka v. U.S. Dept. of Transp., NTSB, 925 F.2d 1147 (9th Cir. 1991)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Multiple procedural issues with evidence: (1) Several foreign language documents (award certificates from Seoul National University and Korean Artist Scholarship Foundation, Biot festival brochure, Korea Daily articles) were submitted without certified English language translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3); (2) Certificate from Les Azuriales Opera Festival was unsigned; (3) Petitioner relied on counsel's assertions without supporting documentary evidence regarding roles and awards, invoking Matter of Soffici that going on record without documentary evidence is insufficient; (4) Key evidence (Julliard faculty appointment and contract addendum dated August 23, 2007) post-dated the petition filing date of February 21, 2007 and could not be considered per Matter of Katigbak requiring eligibility at time of filing; (5) Petitioner's claimed income on Form I-140 was not supported by payroll records, W-2s, or tax returns.
